data.title: Iran latest: Trump tells G7 Iran set to surrender; US aircraft crash kills 4 crew
data.description:
Investing.com - U.S. President Donald Trump told G7 leaders during a virtual meeting this week that Iran is “about to surrender,” according to a report by Axios citing three officials from G7 countries briefed on the call.
[[pro_promotion |
Get latest market-moving news on Iran by upgrading to InvestingPro - off 50% now
]]
During the discussion on Wednesday morning, Trump reportedly told allies he had “got rid of a cancer that was threatening us all,” while highlighting the results of the U.S. military operation known as “Epic Fury.”
According to Axios, Trump also said the situation inside Iran had become so unstable that it was unclear who could formally declare a surrender.
“Nobody knows who is the leader, so there is no one that can announce surrender,” Trump said during the call, the report added.
The comments come as the war in the Middle East approaches its second week, with both sides continuing to exchange drone and missile strikes across the region.
Trump struck an aggressive tone again on Friday, referring to Iran’s leadership as “deranged scumbags” and saying it was his “great honor” to kill them.
Meanwhile, domestic pressure on the administration increased following the loss of a U.S. military aircraft in the region.
U.S. Central Command said a KC-135 refueling aircraft went down in western Iraq at around 2 p.m. Eastern Time on March 12. Four of the six crew members on board have been confirmed dead, while rescue efforts are ongoing.
“The circumstances of the incident are under investigation,” Central Command said in a statement posted on social media, adding that the loss of the aircraft was not caused by hostile fire or friendly fire.
While Iran may be losing in a conventional military sense — something President Donald Trump continues to emphasize — Tehran’s strategy of disrupting the global economy by threatening the Strait of Hormuz and pushing oil prices higher is proving highly effective. Analysts warn that there is no clear military solution to reopening the Strait of Hormuz.
"This is the dilemma facing stocks – while Trump might be looking for an offramp, he is not fully in control of the conflict," Vital Knowledge analyst Adam Crisafulli said in a morning note on Friday.
"The silver lining on Iran is that both sides are holding back, providing room for compromise (or on a darker note, runway for escalation) – the US/Israel have not targeted Iran’s oil infrastructure in a meaningful way (and Iran is still exporting oil from Hormuz) while Iran’s proxy, the Houthis in Yemen, have stayed on the sidelines."

data.title: U.S. futures turn higher as oil prices stay elevated amid Iran conflict
data.description:
Investing.com - Futures linked to the main U.S. stock indices moved higher on Friday, erasing earlier losses, even as oil prices floated above $100 a barrel amid continued fighting in Iran.
[[pro_promotion |
Track U.S. stock futures with InvestingPro
]]
By 06:20 ET (10:20 GMT), the Dow futures contract had risen by 122 points, or 0.3%, S&P 500 futures gained 14 points, or 0.2%, and Nasdaq 100 futures had climbed by 42 points, or 0.2%.
The main averages on Wall Street sank in the previous session, weighed down by few indications that the fighting in the Middle East will soon abate.
Now well over a week old, the joint U.S.-Israeli assault on Iran has shown few signs of abating, with President Donald Trump saying Washington is "totally destroying" Iran's military and economy.
But Tehran has indicated that it will continue to fight. New Iranian Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei has particularly said that the critical Strait of Hormuz waterway, through which a fifth of the world's oil flows, will remain closed.
Although the U.S. and Israel appear to have gained military dominance in their joint campaign, some analysts have suggested that Tehran may be attempting to resist the assault by crimping shipping flows through the strait.
To counteract Iran's hold over the choke point, the U.S. Treasury Department has said it would permit countries to purchase some sanctioned Russian crude until April 11. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent noted as well that the U.S. is planning on the Navy providing an escort for commercial vessels traversing the strait.
U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Gen. Dan Caine, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs, will hold a news conference on Friday morning.
Brent hovers above $100 a barrel
Still, the prospect of a protracted conflict engulfing much of the major oil-producing region of the Middle East has sent Brent crude prices above $100 a barrel.
Brent has been on a wild ride this week. At one point, the global benchmark had soared to as high as almost $120 a barrels, before later retreating briefly below $90 a barrel.
While the extreme fluctuations have captured the headlines, whether the increase will be here to stay has become a central point of debate among investors, analysts at Capital Economics flagged.
"As it stands, investors in the options market put a one-in-five chance of Brent crude prices being $100 per barrel or higher in three months’ time," said Kieran Tompkins, Senior Climate and Commodities Economist at Capital Economics, in a note.
By 04:33 ET on Friday, Brent crude futures had risen by 0.6% to $101.04 a barrel, putting it on track for gain of over 9% in the past one-week period. Prior to the outbreak of the war in Iran, the measure had been trading at roughly $70 a barrel.
PCE data ahead
More detail could come to the inflation picture in the U.S. on Friday, when the personal consumption expenditures price index for January is due out.
Stripping out volatile items like food and fuel, the so-called "core" PCE gauge is seen coming in at 3.1% in the twelve months to January, slightly faster than 3.0% in December. The Commerce Department's measure is closely monitored by financial markets, as it is one of the preferred metrics used by the Fed when determining monetary policy.
Since hitting a low point of 2.6% last summer, the core PCE index has been trending away from the Fed's 2% target, the ING analysts said.
"This limits the ability of the Fed to cut rates this year, and we will hear a lot more from the Fed at next Wednesday's FOMC meeting," they wrote.
On Wednesday, a gauge of consumer price growth from the Labor Department stood at a relatively tame 2.4% year-on-year.
Yet, crucially, the numbers cover a period that largely does not include the Iran war, which began with a barrage of U.S. and Israeli air strikes on Iran in late February. The outlook for inflation has darkened since the outbreak of the fighting.
Elsewhere, a tracker of job openings and labor turnover - a proxy for hiring - for January is also set to be unveiled. Along with inflation, Fed officials have been assessing an American labor market which recent data points have suggested is on shaky ground.
Adobe slides premarket
In individual stocks, shares of Adobe sank by 7.5% in premarket trading after the creative software group said its chief executive of eighteen years, Shantanu Narayen, will step down and that its board had initiated a search for a successor.
Narayen is an Adobe veteran, having joined the company in 1998 and promoted several times until finally taking over as CEO in December 2007. One of Narayen’s biggest moves was bundling the company’s various software products into a cloud-based subscription model.
Under Narayen, Adobe’s annual revenue has surged to a massive $23.77 billion from $3.58 billion, although the group has faced questions recently around disruption from new artificial intelligence-enhanced tools.
But San Jose, California-based Adobe, which is known for its portfolio of software products such as image editor Photoshop and movie maker Premiere Pro, still announced a quarterly top- and bottom-line beat and issued current quarter guidance that was largely above expectations.
Meanwhile, cloud-data management firm Rubrik edged up before the start of the U.S. session on better-than-anticipated fourth-quarter income per share, sales, and subscription annual recurring revenue. Rubrik said the returns demonstrated that the business is "an increasingly critical platform for the AI era."
Beyond software, Ulta Beauty shares also dropped after the cosmetics store chain reported a modest quarterly earnings per share shortfall and underwhelming fiscal 2027 guidance.

data.title: Oil prices float above $100 a barrel amid ongoing Iran war - what’s moving markets
data.description:
Investing.com - Futures linked to the main U.S. stock indices inch lower, as oil prices remain elevated during the continued fighting in the Middle East. Brent crude futures hover above the $100 a barrel level, with few signs of an easing in a joint U.S.-Israeli assault on Iran that has gone on for well over a week. Inflation fears from the oil shock put gold on track for a weekly decline, while more price growth data out of the U.S. is due to be released. Elsewhere, Adobe’s share price slips after the software firm announces the departure of its long-time chief executive.
[[pro_promotion |
Get more market insights with InvestingPro
]]
1. Futures lower
U.S. stock market futures pointed lower on Friday, suggesting a negative end to a trading week that has been roiled by the ongoing Iran war and an attendant oil supply crunch.
By 04:10 ET (08:10 GMT), the Dow futures contract had dropped by 241 points, or 0.5%, S&P 500 futures had slipped by 35 points, or 0.5%, and Nasdaq 100 futures had fallen by 157 points, or 0.6%.
The main averages on Wall Street sank in the previous session, weighed down by few indications that the fighting in the Middle East will soon abate. A statement from new Iranian Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei that the critical Strait of Hormuz waterway will remain closed led to a sustained spike in oil prices that hung over investor sentiment.
Although the U.S. and Israel appear to have gained military dominance in their joint campaign on Iran, some analysts have suggested that Tehran may be attempting to resist the assault by crimping shipping flows through the strait, which hosts a fifth of the world’s oil supply.
To counteract Iran’s hold over the choke point, the U.S. Treasury Department has said it would permit countries to purchase some sanctioned Russian crude until April 11. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent noted as well that the U.S. is planning on the Navy providing an escort for commercial vessels traversing the strait.
2. Brent floats above $100 a barrel
Still, the prospect of a protracted conflict engulfing much of the major oil-producing region of the Middle East has sent Brent crude prices above $100 a barrel.
Brent has been on a wild ride this week. At one point, the global benchmark had soared to as high as almost $120 a barrels, before later retreating briefly below $90 a barrel.
While the extreme fluctuations have captured the headlines, whether the increase will be here to stay has become a central point of debate among investors, analysts at Capital Economics flagged.
"As it stands, investors in the options market put a one-in-five chance of Brent crude prices being $100 per barrel or higher in three months’ time," said Kieran Tompkins, Senior Climate and Commodities Economist at Capital Economics, in a note.
By 04:33 ET on Friday, Brent crude futures had risen by 0.6% to $101.04 a barrel, putting it on track for gain of over 9% in the past one-week period. Prior to the outbreak of the war in Iran, the measure had been trading at roughly $70 a barrel.
3. Gold on track for weekly decline
Spot gold, meanwhile, was on pace for a second straight week of losses, in an indication of worries that the Iran war could spark an energy-fueled inflation spike.
Much of the oil and gas passing through the Strait of Hormuz are used in a range of products, such as fertilizer and plastics, meaning that the sudden uptick in their prices could lead to heavy inflationary pressures in economies around the world.
These fears may translate into central banks, including the Federal Reserve, reconsidering possible interest rate cuts in the near-term. Higher borrowing costs may attract more foreign investment, bolstering the appeal of the U.S. dollar. The dollar index, which track the greenback against a basket of rival currencies, has jumped as the conflict has intensified.
Even though gold is typically seen as a safe haven in times of geopolitical crisis, the yellow metal’s shine has subsequently been marred because a stronger dollar can make bullion more expensive for overseas buyers.
4. PCE ahead
More detail could come to the inflation picture in the U.S. on Friday, when the personal consumption expenditures price index for January is due out.
Stripping out volatile items like food and fuel, the so-called "core" PCE gauge is seen coming in at 3.1% in the twelve months to January, slightly faster than 3.0% in December. The measure is closely monitored by financial markets, as it is one of the preferred metrics used by the Fed when determining monetary policy.
Curiously, the Commerce Department’s PCE readings have recently been hotter than separate official consumer price index growth from the Labor Department, primarily driven by different weighting methods for housing and healthcare, alongside variations in scope and consumer substitution factors. Specifically, the lower weighting of cooling shelter costs in the PCE and higher exposure to rising medical costs have caused the PCE to remain more elevated than CPI.
On Wednesday, February’s CPI was a relatively tame 2.4% year-on-year.
Yet, crucially, the numbers cover a period that largely does not include the Iran war, which began with a barrage of U.S. and Israeli air strikes on Iran in late February. The outlook for inflation has darkened since the outbreak of the fighting.
5. Adobe CEO to step down
Shares of Adobe sank in extended hours trading after the creative software group said its chief executive of eighteen years, Shantanu Narayen, will step down and that its board had initiated a search for a successor.
Narayen is an Adobe veteran, having joined the company in 1998 and promoted several times until finally taking on the helm of chief executive in December 2007. One of Narayen’s biggest moves was bundling the company’s various software products into a cloud-based subscription model.
Under Narayen, Adobe’s annual revenue has surged to a massive $23.77 billion from $3.58 billion.
San Jose, California-based Adobe, which is known for its portfolio of software products such as image editor Photoshop and movie maker Premiere Pro, separately announced a quarterly top- and bottom-line beat and issued current quarter guidance that was largely above expectations.

data.title: Musk’s X submits remedies relating to its blue check mark, EU spokesperson says
data.description: March 13 (Reuters) - Elon Musk’s social media platform X has submitted remedies in relation to its blue checkmark verification feature following a fine of 120 million euros ($137.63 million), a European Commission spokesperson said on Friday. EU tech regulators fined X in December for breaching online content rules, the first such action under a landmark legislation that drew criticism from the U.S. government. Here are the details: * The Commission will now carefully assess the proposedremedies, European Commission spokesperson Thomas Regnier added,without giving details. * X did not immediately respond to Reuters requests forcomment. * The EU action against X had followed a two-year-longinvestigation under the bloc’s Digital Services Act (DSA), whichrequires online platforms to do more to tackle illegal andharmful content. * The European Commission in July 2024 had charged X withdeceiving users, saying that the blue checkmark does notcorrespond to industry practices and that anyone can pay to geta "verified" status. * Bloomberg News first reported on Thursday that X hasagreed to change its verification mechanism in the EuropeanUnion. * The blue checkmark had previously indicated that anaccount belonged to a public figure whose identity was verified,but Musk changed it to indicate it belonged to a paid subscriberafter acquiring X, formerly known as Twitter, in 2022. ($1 = 0.8719 euros)

data.title: German wholesale prices climb 1.2% in February
data.description: Investing.com - German wholesale prices increased 1.2% in February compared with the same month a year earlier, the federal statistics office reported on Friday.The annual rise in wholesale prices marks the latest inflation data point for Europe’s largest economy as it continues to navigate price pressures across various sectors.The statistics office released the February wholesale price index figures, which track the prices charged by wholesalers to retailers and other businesses before goods reach consumers.Wholesale price movements often serve as an indicator of future consumer price trends, as changes at the wholesale level can eventually be passed on to retail customers.This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. For more information see our T&C.

data.title: Glenveagh Properties maintains 2026 guidance with strong order book
data.description: Investing.com - Glenveagh Properties has reaffirmed its fiscal year 2026 guidance, with management expressing continued confidence in the group’s growth trajectory and cash flow generation capabilities.The company maintained its FY26 targets across all divisions. For housebuilding, the company expects 1,600 completions with an average selling price exceeding €375,000 and a gross margin above 21%. The partnerships division is projected to deliver a mid-teen gross margin with average annual gross profit of at least €60 million.Group-wide, Glenveagh anticipates total completions of 2,750 units, €45 million in planned land sales, and earnings per share of 21 cents. The company reported that all units scheduled for delivery in 2026 have commenced construction, with a housebuilding order book of 1,252 units representing 78% of planned deliveries.The company noted that a significant proportion of build costs for the year are already covered, with no changes made to margin guidance. Management acknowledged that earnings will be weighted toward the second half of 2026, reflecting the timing of deliveries from sites acquired in late 2024.Glenveagh reported its land bank requires no further material investment to support annual delivery volumes of 2,750 to 3,600 units through 2030. The company expects meaningful cash generation improvement, particularly in the second half of 2026, driven by increasing delivery volumes and the unwind of €142 million in contract assets.The €25 million share buyback program announced in January is expected to run through approximately May. Management indicated that second half 2026 cash generation will support continued reinvestment and capacity for further capital returns.In the partnerships division, Glenveagh added 100 units to three sites currently in advanced discussions, bringing the total to 500 units. The company disclosed its pipeline for the first time, comprising over 7,000 units with an estimated €3 billion net developable value.This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. For more information see our T&C.

data.title: Vincorion sets share price at €17 ahead of Frankfurt listing
data.description: Investing.com - Defense and aviation supplier Vincorion announced Friday that its planned listing on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange would give the company a total market capitalization of €850 million ($978.6 million).The German company set a sale price of €17 a share and said it will offer investors up to €345 million of shares. The offer period is expected to start on Monday before a planned first day of trading on March 20.Vincorion manufactures hybrid energy systems, microgrids, emergency power generators, and stabilization systems for military platforms including tanks and air defense systems such as the U.S.-made Patriot missiles.Key investors, which include Fidelity International and Invesco Asset Management, have committed to buy around €105 million of shares, the company said.This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. For more information see our T&C.

data.title: Higher gasoline prices could offset a third of tax-cut stimulus, Wolfe says
data.description:
Investing.com - Rising gasoline prices could significantly erode the consumer stimulus expected from the U.S. administration’s One Big Beautiful Bill (OBBB), according to Wolfe Research.
"Low gasoline prices were one of the few pillars supporting the administration’s affordability narrative. That now appears to be unraveling and could potentially offset a material portion of the consumer stimulus from the One Big Beautiful Bill," strategist Stephanie Roth said in a note.
[[pro_promotion |
Dive deeper into energy prices outlook with InvestingPro
]]
Roth compared the impact of higher gasoline prices with the roughly $188 billion in consumer stimulus expected from the tax provisions in the bill. Wolfe’s analysis suggests that while the policy should still provide a net benefit overall, rising fuel costs could dilute its impact on household spending.
“With gasoline prices averaging $3.10 per gallon in 2025, a sustained 20% increase (roughly where prices stand today) would represent about a $65bn annual headwind for consumers,” Roth said.
Under that scenario, she estimates that roughly one-third of the aggregate tax cuts would effectively be offset by higher gasoline expenses. However, the effects would vary significantly across income groups.
The strategist highlights that “the top three income quintiles still see a substantial net benefit, the second-lowest quintile is roughly neutral, and the lowest quintile experiences a clear net loss.”
Lower-income households are particularly vulnerable because they receive limited benefits from the tax cuts while still facing higher fuel costs. Roth noted that “the bottom quintile receives little benefit from the tax cuts but still bears the burden of higher gasoline costs.”
She also modeled more severe energy shock scenarios. If gasoline prices were to rise to $5 per gallon and remain elevated through September, Roth estimates the increase in fuel spending would nearly offset the entire consumer benefit from the tax package. Prices rising to $5.50 over the same period would more than erase the stimulus effect.
"And this is before we incorporate the likely higher food prices as a result of the conflict," Roth added.

data.title: Slowing smartphones, strong gaming: BofA weighs in on consumer electronics
data.description: Investing.com- Bank of America said consumer electronic demand trends diverged sharply across key segments in February, with gaming outperforming and smartphones lagging well below seasonal norms.Smartphone weakness showed few signs of reversing, with U.S. spending in the sector down 2% year-on-year in February. But gaming spending surged 23% in February, BofA said. The outlook for smartphones remained weak, with BofA forecasting that rising memory chip prices were also likely to raise costs and limit margins for manufacturers in the coming months. On the other hand, gaming spending remained solid, with analysts describing the strength as broad-based, driven by sustained consumer engagement rather than seasonal factors alone.The brokerage said Logitech, Soitec, Mycronic, and Nordic Semiconductor together represent the bulk of the roughly 19% consumer exposure across BofA’s European IT hardware coverage.Logitech, according to BofA, was well-exposed to gaming markets and stood to further benefit from strong spending in the United States. Smartphones also showed some pockets of strength, with Apple.com traffic up 4% year-on-year in February, while T-Mobile’s website saw an 8% gain, BofA said.Outside gaming and smartphones, BofA noted some resilience in consumer electronics and hobbies, as the sector struggled to recover from a post-COVID lull.

data.title: US allows countries to buy Russian oil already at sea as Iran war hits supplies
data.description:
Investing.com- The U.S. Treasury on Thursday evening issued a 30-day license allowing countries to buy Russian oil and petroleum goods currently out at sea, in a move aimed at stabilizing global energy markets rattled by the Iran war.
The Treasury’s Office of Foreign Assets Control said in a notice that countries could purchase any Russian oil and petroleum products that were put out to sea before March 12, with the notice valid until April 11. 
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the move aimed at promoting “stability in global energy markets” and to “keep prices low as we address the threat and instability posed by the terrorist Iranian regime.” 
[[pro_promotion |Get more breaking news on oil markets and the Iran crisis by subscribing to InvestingPro]]
Washington had earlier this week loosened some restrictions on Russian oil, especially for major importers such as India, to help offset the impact of the Iran war. 
The U.S. move comes amid heightened hostilities in the U.S.-Israel war with Iran, which saw several tanker attacks and port disruptions in and around the Persian Gulf.
Central to this was the Strait of Hormuz, a key shipping passage for roughly 20% of the world’s oil consumption. Iran was seen attacking international ships around the strait.
Iran’s new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei also said on Thursday that Tehran will keep the strait closed as leverage against the U.S. and Israel.
Oil prices had shot up on the development, with Brent crossing $100/barrel for the second time this week. But prices fell after the U.S. Treasury’s announcement.
